taskDescription: Drag the correct terms into the blanks to complete statements about radiodiagnosis and imaging principles.
textField:
The five radiographic densities from most to least opaque are: *metal*, soft tissue, *fat*, *air*, and foreign body. Dense structures like bone and metal appear *white* on X-ray because they absorb more radiation, while air appears *black*.

Free gas under the diaphragm on an erect abdominal X-ray indicates *perforation* of a hollow viscus and constitutes a *surgical* emergency. This sign is best seen on an erect *chest* X-ray rather than a supine film.

*Barium* sulfate is used to coat the GI mucosa for contrast studies but is contraindicated when *perforation* is suspected, because barium leaking into the peritoneum causes severe chemical peritonitis. In such cases, *water-soluble* iodinated contrast (Gastrografin) is used instead.

CT uses *Hounsfield* units to quantify tissue density. Water measures *zero* HU by definition, bone measures approximately +400 to +1000 HU, air measures *-1000* HU, and fat is typically around -100 HU. CT is the investigation of choice for acute *head* injury and abdominal trauma.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review radiodiagnosis principles and imaging modalities.
Good effort! Revise the contrast agents and density values.
